digest.intro: A first atmosphere, a drowned computer, and compression as art medium.

items.title: The Iran war is reshaping everything — here's the clearest map of it
items.blurb: Hormuz shipping down ~50%, IRGC striking US bases in Kuwait, Bahrain, and Jordan, and war costs now accounting for a third of an $87.6B supplemental funding request — this is not a contained regional flare-up. CSIS's rolling hub aggregates the military, diplomatic, and economic analysis without the churn; the piece on what the war is costing Gulf-US AI ambitions is the sharpest lens on second-order effects right now.

items.title: The American Diabetes Association tried to ambush its own scientists
items.blurb: At the ADA's June annual meeting, five scientists were ejected for handing out copies of a published editorial criticizing Trump-era attacks on research — and new details suggest ADA leadership pre-arranged the venue security and police response as a deliberate ambush. The deputy editors of the ADA's own journal then tried to publish seven follow-up pieces; the ADA refused. They're now on a preprint server, which is a remarkable thing to do to your own editorial board.

sections.heading: Signal from space and from deep time

items.title: First confirmed atmosphere on a rocky, habitable-zone exoplanet
items.blurb: JWST spectroscopy detected helium in the atmosphere of LHS 1140 b — a rocky planet 48 light-years away orbiting in the Goldilocks zone of a red dwarf. Helium alone doesn't support life, but its presence means the planet has held onto *something* against stellar radiation long enough to matter, and other gases may follow in subsequent observations. Of 6,000+ known exoplanets, this is the first time anyone has confirmed an atmosphere on a rocky world in a habitable zone.

items.title: The computer at the bottom of a canal — and why it was right about everything
items.blurb: In 1988, Linn Products — the Glasgow hi-fi company that made the Sondek LP12 turntable — built custom silicon that checked memory bounds in hardware, garbage-collected its own heap in silicon, and treated memory and disk as a single persistent object store. The chip ended up in the Forth and Clyde Canal; the ideas it embodied are now shipping in production ARM silicon in 2026. A perfect object-with-a-life essay about being right in the wrong decade.

items.title: Regressive JPEGs: iterative compression as a generative medium
items.blurb: Progressive JPEG stores image data in multiple scan passes — DC coefficients first, then successively finer frequency bins — and this project runs that process in reverse and iteratively, using each compression artifact as the input to the next pass. The result is a controlled descent into Fourier-space hallucination, where the image structure slowly dissolves into its own low-frequency ghost. It's compression as erosion, and the outputs look like nothing else.

items.title: Cross-sectioning crickets inside a scanning electron microscope with a femtosecond laser
items.blurb: Femtosecond lasers ablate material without heating it — the pulses are so short the energy has no time to conduct — which lets Ben Krasnow cut clean cross-sections of chemically fixed, solvent-exchanged, argon-charred crickets while they sit inside a live SEM. One unexpected problem: the paint on his shutter was getting coated with charged carbon, forming a capacitor that flooded the electron image with noise. The resulting insect cross-sections are stunning and deeply strange.

items.title: Why every AI company logo looks like an aperture — or a butthole
items.blurb: The radial symmetry, central void, and soft gradient that define OpenAI, Gemini, Anthropic, and a dozen others aren't accidents — they're the visual language of 'intelligence' as an industry has decided to signal it. The essay traces the convergence through corporate design-speak (OpenAI's official explanation calls their logo 'the dynamic intersection between humanity and technology') and lands on a real point: when every company is reaching for the same metaphor of aperture-as-mind, the whole category becomes illegible.

items.title: Vāgdhenu: a TTS engine built for Vedic Sanskrit chanting, meter and all
items.blurb: Standard TTS destroys Sanskrit chanting because it ignores svara (tonal accent) and vṛtta (meter) — the prosodic rules that distinguish a recitation from a reading. This system, built at IISc Bengaluru, auto-detects meter from any Indian script input and renders a meter-aware chanted output, trained on a purpose-recorded single-speaker corpus with a fine-tuned neural vocoder. It now powers the complete 12-skandha Śrīmad Bhāgavatam app with synced karaoke highlighting — software as oral tradition preservation.

facts.fact: The US-Iran war has pushed Hormuz shipping volume down roughly 50%, and war costs now account for one-third of an $87.6 billion supplemental funding request to Congress.
LHS 1140 b is the first rocky exoplanet in a star's habitable zone ever confirmed to have an atmosphere — detected by JWST spectroscopy — out of more than 6,000 known exoplanets.

Linn Products, best known for the Sondek LP12 turntable, designed a processor in 1988 that garbage-collected its own heap in hardware and treated RAM and disk as a single persistent object store — ideas now implemented in production ARM silicon nearly 40 years later.

Painting both sides of railroad rail white drops rail surface temperature by roughly 20°F, enough to meaningfully reduce the risk of thermal misalignment — and Union Pacific says no other US railroad is currently doing it.

To prepare crickets for femtosecond laser cross-sectioning inside an SEM, they must be soaked in glutaraldehyde to cross-link proteins, solvent-exchanged to remove water, and then charred under argon to make them electrically conductive.

make_something.prompt: Take a single image — a photo you took, anything — and write a script that saves it as a progressive JPEG, then re-loads and re-saves it 30–50 times in a loop, each time reducing quality by one notch. Export every 5th iteration as a frame and assemble them into a short video or flipbook. You're not degrading the image; you're watching it fall back through its own frequency decomposition. Pay attention to which structures survive longest.

art_picks.title: Sans Soleil
art_picks.creator: Chris Marker
art_picks.why: A film that operates as a meditation on memory, ritual, and how images construct reality — Marker drifts between Tokyo, Guinea-Bissau, and Iceland, building a theory of time and forgetting through essayistic montage. Structurally strange, obsessively crafted, impossible to shake. Exactly the kind of deep-systems-meets-expressive-surface work that earns repeat viewings.

art_picks.title: UUmwelt
art_picks.creator: Pierre Huyghe
art_picks.why: Machine learning trained on recorded brain activity reconstructs imagined images in real time on freestanding screens in MoMA's Sculpture Garden — and the gazes of visitors actively alter the output. It's a living feedback loop between human cognition and nonhuman intelligence, a system that runs on attention itself. Huyghe's framing — 'a collective production of imagination between two kinds of intelligences' — is the kind of proposition that makes you want to go make something.

art_picks.title: Selections from 'Vespertine'
art_picks.creator: Björk
art_picks.why: An album built like a miniature architecture — microbeats, music boxes, harps, choirs, and found domestic sounds (ice cracking, cutlery) layered into something that feels simultaneously ancient and digital. The physical/digital edge made into music: intimate systems at maximum resolution. If you haven't listened with headphones in a quiet room in a while, now is the time.

events.name: Yves Saint Laurent and Photography
events.venue: International Center of Photography, 84 Ludlow St, Lower East Side
events.why: Nearly 300 works showing how photography was the actual technology of myth-making for YSL — contact sheets, Avedon, Newton, Klein, Warhol portraits — a case study in how image systems construct cultural identity.

events.name: Pierre Huyghe: UUmwelt
events.venue: MoMA Abby Aldrich Rockefeller Sculpture Garden, 11 West 53rd St, Manhattan
events.why: A generative AI installation activated in real time by visitors' gazes and cancer-cell mutation data — art as living ecosystem, running outdoors in MoMA's garden all summer.

events.name: Anjunadeep Open Air New York City
events.venue: Central Park SummerStage, Rumsey Playfield, 69th St & 5th Ave
events.why: Seven hours of melodic progressive house in Central Park — James Grant & Jody Wisternoff, Marsh, Durante, Because of Art — a rare label showcase that treats the outdoor setting as part of the aesthetic rather than just a venue.

events.name: MoMA PS1 Warm Up 2026 — Opening Night
events.venue: MoMA PS1 Courtyard, 22-25 Jackson Ave, Long Island City, Queens
events.why: Opening night of PS1's 28th-season outdoor series features Carlos Souffront, a Wackies dub showcase with Bronx reggae founder Lloyd Barnes b2b Basic Channel's Mark Ernestus, and TELESONIQ — diasporic club histories colliding in a courtyard surrounded by Greater New York 2026 and Precious Okoyomon's living forest installation.

events.name: UBB 40th Anniversary Celebration (SummerStage)
events.why: A free reunion of foundational hip-hop: BreakBeat Lou & Lord Finesse, Diamond D, Grand Puba, Sadat X, Nice & Smooth — a living document of a scene before it hardened into an institution, free and in the park.
